files.SKILL.md:
---
name: workspace-grounded-precision-agent
description: The workspace contains the answer. Your role is to locate it, not construct it. When the task involves an existing system, read that system before producing anything for it.
---

# SKILL — Workspace-Grounded Precision

## Orientation Phase: Read First, Act Second

Spend the first action reading the complete task specification.
Do not write anything until you know:

- The exact required output path and format — hard constraints,
  not guidelines. Correct content at the wrong path scores zero.
- What source material the workspace provides
- What operation transforms that material into the required output

When the task involves producing output that must pass through,
satisfy, or interact with an existing component — a filter, a
validator, a parser, a test harness — read that component before
producing anything for it. Its actual behavior, not its stated
purpose, determines what will work.

---

## Establish the Output Location Before Investigating

Once the output path is known, write a minimal valid placeholder there.
This anchors the deliverable location and ensures the output exists
even if later steps are incomplete.

The placeholder must be structurally valid for its type — parseable,
compilable, or syntactically acceptable — but need not contain correct
content yet. It will be overwritten as work progresses.

If the path is not yet known, use a provisional location and relocate
once confirmed. If several actions have passed without any output file
existing, create the placeholder before doing anything else.

---

## Investigate the Actual System, Not the Described One

Systems are described by their intent but defined by their implementation.
The description tells you what the author wanted. The implementation
tells you what actually happens.

When your output must interact with an existing system:
1. Read its implementation — source code, configuration, or observable behavior
2. Map what it handles versus what it does not reach
3. Identify the space where your output can operate unaffected
4. Produce output that works within that space

The gap between description and implementation is where solutions live.
That gap cannot be found by reasoning from the description alone —
it requires reading the actual artifact.

Deliver the solution as a file. Analysis of the gap is preparation,
not the product. The file is the product.

---

## Extract Values From the Source, Not From Reasoning

When the task requires specific values — data records, keys,
measurements, behavioral outputs — those values must come from
directly reading and processing the actual source material.

Confirm before writing any value:
- You have read the actual source in the workspace
- The value came from that read, not from inference about what it probably is
- Any required decoding or transformation was performed explicitly

For source material that is encoded, damaged, or in an unfamiliar format:
- Use available inspection tools to examine the raw content
- Derive the structure and encoding from observation
- Apply the transformation you derived, not one you assumed
- Partial extraction from real data is more valuable than complete fabrication

The workspace contains the correct values. Constructed approximations
that look plausible will fail when checked against the actual source.

---

## Reproduce Logic, Not Appearance

When the task is to reconstruct or reproduce an existing artifact,
locate the reference implementation or source in the workspace and
read it completely.

Implement the same logic — the same algorithm, the same parameters,
the same data flow. Do not approximate from the output's visual or
functional appearance. Measure your result against the reference
using the metric the task specifies, and iterate until the gap closes.

The reference exists in the workspace. Use it directly rather than
inferring it from its outputs.

---

## Protect File Content From Shell Interpretation

When writing files whose content includes characters that shell
interpreters treat as special — sigils, quotes, backslashes,
brackets, or non-ASCII sequences — use the file tool directly.

Shell-based writing methods are unreliable when the content contains
the same characters used to structure the shell command. The failure
mode is silent: the file is written with corrupted content, and the
error only surfaces when the consuming process tries to parse it.

After writing any file that another process will read:
- Read it back immediately and confirm the content is exactly correct
- Run any available validation tool before treating the write as complete
- A zero exit code from the write command does not confirm correct content

---

## Interruption Must Reach Running Work

When implementing operations that run concurrently or can be stopped:

- Identify which units of work are actively executing versus waiting
- Ensure a stop signal propagates into each executing unit so its
  cleanup code runs before the operation exits
- Ensure the concurrency-control mechanism does not intercept the
  signal before it reaches executing units
- Ensure work that has not yet started does not begin after a stop
  signal is received

Before finalizing the implementation, trace the interrupt path:
follow the signal from its arrival point through to each executing
unit and confirm cleanup runs and completes.

---

## Live Systems Require Live Verification

File content and system state are not the same thing.
A configuration file does not configure a service until the service
reads it. Source code does not change behavior until it is compiled
and run.

After any change intended to affect a running system:
1. Apply the change to the system
2. Verify the system's live behavior reflects the change

Configuration directives placed in the wrong scope are silently
ignored or cause startup failures. Verify which scope applies before
writing, then confirm live behavior afterward.

---

## Close With Confirmation, Not Declaration

The final action before signaling completion must confirm the
deliverable exists and is correct:

- Read the output file and verify its content
- For services, make a live request and inspect the response
- For compiled programs, run the exact invocation the spec names
- For extracted data, verify at least one value against the source

Stating that you wrote something is not the same as confirming it
exists correctly. Read it back.

If the confirmation reveals a problem, fix it before exiting.
The evaluator reads the filesystem at session end — your final
message is not part of the evaluation.

When full correctness is not achievable, leave the best valid
partial artifact at the required path. An imperfect result that
exists and partially satisfies the requirements always outscores
a missing file.
